Tafseer:
The meaning of 'Tin' is Mount Tin:
'Wa' (By) is for an oath, and we repeatedly clarify that oaths by objects and places in the Qur'an are sworn to serve as proofs of the claim coming after them. Here, 'Tin' does not refer to the well-known fruit, the fig, as commentators have assumed; rather, it refers to Mount Tin, which was famous for the cultivation of figs. Our teacher (may Allah have mercy on him) explained this in his commentary, and we present the essential part here. He wrote:
'"Tin" is the name of a specific mountain. In Arabic, figs are called tin. Since there was an abundance of fig cultivation here, the mountain became famous by this name. The Arabs knew it by this name, which was a common naming practice among them. When a place was famous for a specific cultivation, they would name it after that product—such as "Ghada", "Shajarah", "Nakhlah", etc.'
'The famous poet, Nabighah al-Dhubyani, mentioned "Tin" as a place in his verses:
"The red clouds approached Tin from its side,
driving along a cloud containing little cold water,"
where he used "Tin" to refer to a mountain in the north. Some have said it is located between Hulwan and Hamadan.'
Further on, refuting other assumptions, he stated his definitive opinion:
'This shows that "Tin" refers either to Mount Judi or to another mountain near it. The Torah states that after the flood of Noah, the children of Adam dispersed from here, and the Qur'an indicates that this occurred near Mount Judi.'
'Zaytun' refers to the Mount of Olives: 'Zaytun' does not refer to the olive tree or its fruit, as commentators have assumed; rather, it refers to the Mount of Olives, which is well known as the center of Jesus' call and worship, and is mentioned repeatedly in the Gospels.
Our teacher wrote in his commentary:
'In our view, this is also the name of a place. Since olive cultivation was abundant here, it was named Zaytun in accordance with the Arabs' naming practice. This is undoubtedly the mountain mentioned in the Gospels, where Jesus (peace be upon him) went to pray. In Luke 21:37, it is mentioned:
"And in the daytime he was teaching in the temple; and at night he went out, and abode in the mount that is called the Mount of Olives."
This opinion is supported by the statements of the predecessors. Ibn Abbas and Ka'b narrated that "Zaytun" refers to Jerusalem, and Qatadah said it is the mountain where Jerusalem is situated.'
The testimony of Mount Tin to the Recompense:
First, an oath is sworn by Mount Tin, and in the light of proofs, it refers to Mount Judi. Two major events of Allah's law of retribution occurred near this mountain, detailed in the ancient scriptures: the event of Adam (peace be upon him), and the event of Noah (peace be upon him) and his people. Regarding the first, our teacher wrote:
'"Tin" is the first place where the first event of recompense occurred from Allah's side. The detail is: when Adam (peace be upon him) forgot God's covenant and, falling into the trap of his envious enemy, ate the fruit of the forbidden tree, they had to face the law of recompense. The honor Allah had bestowed was stripped from them, and the garments of Paradise were taken away... and this became an eternal reminder for their entire descendants. Thus, the Qur'an repeatedly recalls this from this perspective—for example:
(O children of Adam, let not Satan tempt you as he removed your parents from Paradise, stripping them of their clothing) (Al-A'raf, 7:27).'
'Keep in mind what is mentioned in the Torah—namely: when Adam and Eve (peace be upon them) were stripped of their clothing, they covered themselves with the leaves of the fig tree.'
'After this event, the Qur'an states that Adam and Eve repented, Allah accepted their repentance, and promised to reveal guidance and reward those who followed it. This was the second covenant Allah made with Adam after the first. This shows that the event of Mount Tin has two different aspects: Allah stripped a blessing because he forgot the covenant, and bestowed a great blessing because they took heed and repented.'
The second event of recompense near Mount Tin occurred in the era of Noah (peace be upon him). Our teacher described it:
'In his era, Allah Almighty destroyed the wrongdoers near this mountain, and saved the righteous from the flood. In the Holy Qur'an, He states:
(And it was said, "O earth, swallow your water, and O sky, withhold [your rain]." And the water subsided, and the matter was accomplished, and the ark came to rest on [Mount] Judi. And it was said, "Away with the wrongdoing people.") (Hud, 11:44).
And after Noah's supplication, he was directed:
(It was said, "O Noah, disembark in security from Us and blessings upon you and upon nations [descending] from those with you. But other nations We will grant enjoyment; then there will touch them from Us a painful punishment.") (Hud, 11:48).
...This shows that Mount Tin is a historical monument of the manifestation of Allah's law of retribution.'

Mount Sinai: That these refer to places is clear, but regarding 'Seeneen' (Sinai), our teacher's research is:
'In one place, "Toori Seena'" (Mount Sinai) (Al-Mu'minun, 23:20) appeared—meaning in one place it is feminine, and in another, it is in the plural form. Just as in Arabic, "jam'an" and "ajma'oon" are used. The Torah uses "Sinai" in some places, and "Seenim" in others, and in Hebrew, "im" is the plural marker.' (Footnote 1)
The testimony of Mount Sinai to the Recompense:
Describing the testimony of Mount Sinai, our teacher wrote:
'The testimony of Mount Sinai to the Recompense is entirely clear. This is the place where Allah Almighty showed His favor to an oppressed and weak nation, saving them from the grasp of their enemies because of their patience, and then blessing them with a Shariah that was a punishment for the deniers and enemies. This is a clear example of favor upon the oppressed and wrath upon the wrongdoers, as indicated in various places of the Qur'an. For example:
(And the good word of your Lord was fulfilled for the Children of Israel because of what they patiently endured. And We destroyed what Pharaoh and his people were making and what they had been erecting.) (Al-A'raf, 7:137).'
Our teacher explained these events in detail, but since they are well known, we present only these brief excerpts.
_____
Footnote 1: We have explained that in Arabic, the plural is sometimes used to show the vastness of the corners of something; it seems this rule exists in Hebrew as well.

'The secure city' refers to Mecca. Why did He refer to it by its attribute instead of naming it? We will answer this when explaining the relationship of the oaths with the subject.
The testimony of the secure city to the Recompense:
Mecca is the secure city. Allah made it a safe sanctuary, as He stated:
(And whoever enters it shall be safe) (Al-Imran, 3:97).
When Abraham (peace be upon him) migrated from his polytheistic people to this region, it was completely uncultivated and insecure. Abraham prayed for its sustenance and peace, which Allah Almighty accepted—blessing it with abundance of sustenance and security. And both blessings were granted to the people through the Kaaba Abraham constructed. Thus, He stated:
(So let them worship the Lord of this House, Who has fed them, [saving them] from hunger, and made them safe, [saving them] from fear.) (Quraysh, 106:3-4).
This favor was granted to Abraham because of the sacrifices he offered in the path of monotheism. When he succeeded in the greatest test—the sacrifice of his son—Allah Almighty blessed him with the leadership of nations. Abraham asked if this promise of leadership applied to his descendants as well, and Allah answered: 'My covenant does not include the wrongdoers.' That is: this is the reward for your sacrifices; therefore, only those of your descendants who follow your way will share in it, while those who deviate will face the consequence in accordance with the law of recompense, as stated:
(And [remember] when Abraham was tried by his Lord with commands and he fulfilled them. [Allah] said, 'Indeed, I will make you as a leader for the people.' [Abraham] said, 'And of my descendants?' [Allah] said, 'My covenant does not include the wrongdoers.') (Al-Baqarah, 2:124).
This shows that this place is not only a manifestation of Allah's law of retribution, but is the place from which He announced His law: who will deserve His favor and who will face His wrath.
Answering a question regarding the order of names: Our teacher answered this question, writing:
'The order is based on the similarity of the events. First, the event of Adam is mentioned due to its chronological precedence. Next, the event of Jesus is mentioned due to the similarity between Adam and Jesus, which the Qur'an clarified as:
(Indeed, the likeness of Jesus with Allah is as the likeness of Adam) (Al-Imran, 3:59).'
'Next, the two places related to Moses and Muhammad (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) are mentioned due to the similarity between them, which the Qur'an clarified as:
(Indeed, We have sent to you a Messenger as a witness over you just as We sent to Pharaoh a messenger) (Al-Muzzammil, 73:15).'
'This similarity is also present in the prophecy of the Torah:
"I will raise up for them a prophet like you from among their brothers, and I will put My words in his mouth, and he will tell them everything I command him. And if anyone does not listen to My words which he speaks in My name, I will hold him accountable."'

The primary claim proved by the oaths:
This is the primary claim proved by the oaths. It is stated: We created man in the best of molds, but Our law is that those who appreciate this favor, preserve the guidance endowed within their nature, and accept the Messenger's call to lead a life of faith and righteous deeds will receive His eternal reward; and those who do not will be deprived of faith and righteous deeds, and We will cast them into the lowest of the low—from which He intended to protect them through this favor.
'We have certainly created man in the best of molds' (laqad khalaqna-l-insana fi ahsani taqweem) - 'Taqweem' literally means to straighten, as in: 'I straightened the spear and it became straight.' Metaphorically, it means making something suitable for a specific purpose. Man has been blessed with superior capabilities for a great purpose: the Qur'an repeatedly clarifies that man was not created in vain, but with a great purpose (bi-l-haqq)—namely: in this world of trial, he must resist the false temptations of Satan and walk on the straight path. If he does so, Allah Almighty will bless him with His eternal kingdom; and if he is misled by Satan to leave the straight path, He will leave him to wander in the valley of ruin. Regarding this purpose, He created man in the best of molds. His physical form bears witness that he is the most noble of creation, and his spiritual and intellectual capabilities are such that he alone is worthy of being made God's representative on earth, and if he fulfills this role, being made worthy of the eternal kingdom. The previous Surahs explained that He endowed man with the capacity to distinguish between good and bad, and to love goodness and loathe evil. Furthermore, He made him a rational and willing being, unlike other creations. All of this bears witness that He has blessed him with the capabilities necessary to fulfill the purpose of his creation.

The law under which Allah deals with man: 'Then We return him to the lowest of the low' (thumma radadnahu asfala safileen) - This points to the law under which He deals with man. It is stated: since man is a willing being, his enjoying this status of the best of molds or being deprived of it depends on his own attitude. If he appreciates it, He elevates his status; and if he does not, but inclines toward the low, He returns him to the low, until he falls into the lowest level of Hell.
In my view, 'asfala' is an adverb and 'safileen' is a circumstantial clause derived from the pronoun of 'radadnahu' (We return him)—showing that Allah Almighty casts him to the low because he prefers the low, lacking the courage to ascend to the heights.
It may be asked how the plural 'safileen' can serve as a circumstantial clause for a singular pronoun, but this doubt is incorrect. Although the pronoun is singular, its reference is 'man' (al-insan), which carries a plural meaning; hence, the Qur'an uses both singular and plural pronouns for it.

The attributes of those who are saved from ruin: 'Except those who believe and do righteous deeds, for they will have a reward uninterrupted' (illa-lladhina amanu wa 'amilu-s-salihati fa-lahum ajrun ghayru mamnun) - This describes those whom He protects from this ruin: those who appreciate being created in the best of molds, choose a life of faith and righteous deeds, will be elevated in status and blessed with His eternal reward.
'Ghayra mamnun' means uninterrupted and eternal. Some have interpreted it otherwise, but that is contrary to Arabic grammar.
Having determined the primary claim, let us reflect on how the oaths prove this claim.

'Then what causes you, [O man], to deny the Recompense after [this]?' (fa-ma yukadhdhibuka ba'du bi-d-deen) - Our teacher (may Allah have mercy on him) explained this in his commentary:
'Regarding "Then what causes you to deny", there are two opinions:
One is: O man, after these clear testimonies, what is it that makes you deny the Recompense? This was preferred by Mujahid. When it was said to him that the addressee is the Prophet, he said: "God forbid! The addressee is man." Al-Zamakhshari preferred this, taking "yukadhdhibu" in the meaning of "inciting to deny". If this meaning is established, this interpretation is clear, but he presented no proof for it.'
'The other is: O Prophet, after this, what is it that denies you regarding the Recompense! Al-Farra' preferred this. While it does not depart from the common meaning of the words, keeping in view the context and interrogative style, it does not seem correct. First, there is no reason to address the Prophet with two interrogative clauses here; second, the force of "fa-ma yukadhdhibu" and the emphasis of "ba'du" are completely lost in this interpretation. The context and structural coherence support the interpretation of Mujahid, where the words retain their actual meaning, and two excellent interpretations are generated.'
'One is: O man, after these testimonies and proofs, what other proof is there that denies your belief in the Recompense? In this case, the addressee is man, and those who believe in the Recompense are supported, while those who are in doubt are stimulated to reflect.'
'Through the word "ma" (what), reference is made to the reality that man has always chosen denial out of imitation and stubbornness. There is no proof to support him in this path. Not a single thing in the universe denies the Recompense. Therefore, man is invited to set aside imitation, reflect on the proofs, and see if there is anything that denies the Recompense.'
'The other is: after these testimonies, what are the delusions and desires that deceive man regarding the Recompense?'
'In this case, the focus is on the deniers. A similar address appeared in Surah Al-Infitar:
(O man, what has deceived you concerning your Lord, the Generous?) (Al-Infitar, 82:6).'
Our teacher explained the objective of these two questions:
'Ponder over the objective of both questions: in both interpretations, the objective of the first is that after these many proofs of recompense are presented, man should accept it and protect himself from the doubts arising from others or from his own soul.'

Our teacher (may Allah have mercy on him) wrote:
'The objective of the second question "Is not Allah the most just of judges?" (alaysa-llahu bi-ahkami-l-hakimeen) is that they should accept the Recompense because it is His attribute. The meaning is: is not Allah the most just of judges? How can He leave humans unbridled, without distinguishing between the good and bad!
(Then will We treat the Muslims like the criminals? What is the matter with you? How do you judge?) (Al-Qalam, 68:35-36).'
Note that the mention of man being created in the 'best of molds' means He endowed him with the capacity to distinguish between good and bad, and to love justice and loathe injustice. This requires that the Creator Himself possesses this love for justice and loathing for injustice in His perfect form. From this also arises the necessity of a Day of Justice to judge His creation. If He did not do so, He would not be 'the most just of judges', whereas He is indeed the most just of judges, and His attribute cannot be denied.
